ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (lamb, grilled) used as closest match; no specific code exists for Indian-spiced adraki lamb chops in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Indian-spiced lamb chop preparation; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 pieces of lamb chop weighing approximately 250 g, as listed on the GrabFood menu for this dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Lamb, domestic, loin chop, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, cooked, broiled (FDC ID 174351); supplemented with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for spice-marinated lamb preparations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (protein, fat, energy) anchored to USDA FDC broiled lamb loin chop (FDC ID 174351). Sodium elevated to 340 mg/100 g to reflect double marinade with salt and spices, consistent with ICMR-NIN data for spiced lamb preparations. Carbohydrate (4 g/100 g) reflects yogurt and spice marinade contribution. Input values cross-checked against ICMR-NIN Table 1 (lamb/mutton, cooked). Values are plausible and retained from input with source anchoring.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous-food lookup for high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for adraki lamb chops. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled meat entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is 4 g/100 g; at 250 g serving, available carbohydrate ≈ 10 g. GL = (15 × 10) / 100 = 1.5 per serving. This is consistent with the low-GI classification for protein-dominant meat dishes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 340 mg/100 g; saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 340 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management; a 250 g serving delivers 850 mg sodium (37% of the 2300 mg daily limit), so patients should avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as pickles or chutneys and limit marinade salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4 g/100 g), high protein (18 g/100 g), estimated GI 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 4 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 15, this dish has minimal glycaemic impact; patients with type 2 diabetes should pair it with non-starchy vegetables rather than naan or white rice to keep the overall meal glycaemic load low.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g; cholesterol 60 mg/100 g; sodium 340 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 7.2 g/100 g exceeds the AHA threshold of 5 g/100 g for cardiovascular concern; a 250 g serving provides 18 g saturated fat, which is substantial relative to the 13 g daily limit, so cardiovascular patients should limit portion size to one piece and choose leaner cuts where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (lamb) rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); saturated fat 7.2 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Lamb is a red meat RA hard-gate trigger due to high arachidonic acid content that amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute lamb with chicken or fish while retaining the anti-inflammatory ginger and turmeric marinade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adraki Lamb Chops (Adraki Chop) is a Singaporean Indian restaurant dish consisting of two bone-in lamb chops that are double-marinated in a mixture of fresh ginger (adrak), garlic, plain yogurt, and a blend of Indian spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, chilli powder, and turmeric, then grilled or pan-fried until cooked through with a lightly charred exterior. The name 'adraki' derives from the Hindi word for ginger, which is the dominant flavour note. The dish is tender and juicy with a warm, aromatic spice profile, a slight heat from chilli, and the characteristic tang of yogurt tenderising the lamb. It is served as a starter or main course in Indian restaurants across Singapore and is typically accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 232 kcal, 18 g protein, 16 g fat (of which 7.2 g is saturated), and only 4 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with minimal glycaemic impact (estimated GI 15). The dish is halal and gluten-free. Clinically, the saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis patients, while the low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for type 2 diabetes management when paired with non-starchy sides.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat adraki lamb chops?
Ginger lamb chop Indian restaurant Singapore

ingredient_synonyms.mutton: lamb (used interchangeably in South Asian culinary context)

confidence_reason: Core macros are anchored to USDA FoodData Central broiled lamb loin chop (FDC ID 174351) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN data for spiced lamb preparations. No exact match for adraki double-marinated lamb chops exists in any approved primary source; values are adapted estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for high-protein grilled meat. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific double-marinated Indian-spiced lamb chop preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN published tables as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.2 g/100 g consistent with naturally occurring trans fats (vaccenic acid, conjugated linoleic acid) present in ruminant meat; this is not an industrial trans fat. Ethnic tags 'Naan' and 'Biryani' from the input record were removed as they are dish names, not ethnic identifiers. The dish is classified as a Singaporean Indian restaurant preparation; multilingual names follow the Singaporean dish mandatory language rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for adraki lamb chops was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
